coverage_notes: Strong coverage across menstrual cycle effects on performance (3 papers including meta-analyses), postpartum return to running (3 papers including Delphi consensus statements and scoping review), pregnancy exercise guidelines (2 papers including ACOG position statement), iron deficiency in female athletes (2 papers), sex differences in endurance (2 papers including VO2max review), and energy availability/RED-S (1 paper). Minor gap: oral contraceptive effects covered as companion to menstrual cycle meta-analysis. Could benefit from additional papers on bone stress injuries in female runners and pelvic floor dysfunction.

papers.title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Exercise Performance in Eumenorrheic Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
papers.notes: Landmark meta-analysis finding exercise performance may be trivially reduced during early follicular phase compared to all other phases, but effect size is trivial with large between-study variation. Recommends individualized rather than generalized approach to menstrual cycle-based training. Included both endurance and strength outcomes.

papers.title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Elite Athlete Performance: A Critical and Systematic Review
papers.notes: Systematic review focused specifically on elite athletes finding variable associations between menstrual cycle phase and performance outcomes including endurance and power. Female athletes perceive performance to be worse during early follicular and late luteal phases, but objective studies do not show clear, consistent effects.

papers.title: The Effects of Oral Contraceptives on Exercise Performance in Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
papers.notes: Companion meta-analysis to McNulty 2020, examining oral contraceptive pill (OCP) effects on exercise performance. Found OCP use might result in slightly inferior performance compared to naturally menstruating women, but group-level effects are most likely trivial. Current evidence does not warrant general guidance against OCP use for athletes.

papers.title: Effects of Follicular and Luteal Phase-Based Menstrual Cycle Resistance Training on Muscle Strength and Mass
papers.notes: Critical review of menstrual cycle phase-based periodization for resistance training. Finds follicular phase-based resistance training may be superior to luteal phase-based training for building strength and muscle mass, though evidence base is limited. Important for female runners incorporating strength work into training.

papers.title: Physical Activity and Exercise During Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period: ACOG Committee Opinion, Number 804
papers.notes: Definitive ACOG clinical guidance on exercise during pregnancy and postpartum. Recommends women who were physically active before pregnancy can continue vigorous-intensity activity. States physical activity in pregnancy is safe and desirable in absence of obstetric complications, with benefits including reduced gestational diabetes and faster postpartum recovery.

papers.title: Physical activity during pregnancy: a systematic review for the assessment of current evidence with future recommendations
papers.journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
papers.notes: Systematic review of RCTs on exercise during pregnancy. Recommends moderate intensity activities throughout pregnancy, with running and yoga suitable in first trimester, transitioning to aquatic exercise and walking as pregnancy progresses. Exercise 35-90 min 3-4 times/week not associated with increased preterm birth risk.

papers.title: Infographic. Guidance for medical, health and fitness professionals to support women in returning to running postnatally
papers.notes: The peer-reviewed BJSM publication of the landmark 2019 Goom/Donnelly/Brockwell postnatal running guidelines. Recommends no running before 12 weeks postpartum, pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment before returning, and criteria-based progression including strength screening, impact drills, and symptom monitoring. First guidelines specifically for postnatal runners.

papers.title: Clinical and exercise professional opinion on designing a postpartum return-to-running training programme: an international Delphi study and consensus statement
papers.notes: International Delphi consensus on designing postpartum return-to-running programmes. Key recommendations include individualised exercise prescription, gradual activity increases, walk-run protocols, and targeted muscle strengthening. Training should be modified based on musculoskeletal/pelvic symptoms, sleep, mental health, lactation, and energy availability.

papers.title: Navigating the 'new normal': what guidelines exist for postpartum return to physical activity and sport? A scoping review
papers.notes: Scoping review identifying a lack of consistent, evidence-based guidelines for postpartum return to activity/sport. Highlights multiple evidence gaps requiring additional research to inform patient- and activity-specific guidelines. Important for understanding the current state of postpartum exercise science.

papers.title: Iron deficiency, supplementation, and sports performance in female athletes: A systematic review
papers.journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
papers.notes: Systematic review finding up to 60% of female athletes experience iron deficiency, with endurance performance negatively affected by 3-4%. Iron supplementation (~100mg elemental iron/day) improved performance by 2-20% in iron-deficient athletes. Recommends consultation with sports medicine practitioners for supplementation protocols.

papers.title: Iron considerations for the athlete: a narrative review
papers.notes: Comprehensive narrative review covering iron's role in athletic performance, with prevalence of iron deficiency at 15-35% in female athletes vs 5-11% in males. Examines impact of sex hormones, diet, training, altitude, and exercise-induced hepcidin elevation on iron metabolism. Provides practical recommendations for managing iron status in endurance athletes.

papers.title: Sex Differences in Endurance Running
papers.notes: Comprehensive review of sex differences in endurance running covering the 10-12% performance gap. Males have higher hemoglobin, larger hearts/lungs, and greater VO2max. Females show advantages in type I fibre proportion, fat oxidation, and pacing strategy. Sex-specific biomechanical differences exist including greater non-sagittal hip/knee motion in females. Important for understanding why coaching approaches may need sex-specific adjustments.
